source_signals.title: Trump’s new budget ignores dying Americans and gives away record sums to the US military
source_signals.summary: President’s proposed budget slashes health department by 12% while throwing $1.5tn – a 42% increase – to the military Americans are dying in droves. Deaths due to avoidable causes in the United States –which could be dealt with via prevention or proper healthcare – far outpace those in most of country’s peers in the industrialized world. Most notably, Americans die of treatable conditions at nearly twice the rate as Spaniards, French, Japanese and Australians. They would most likely live longer if they enjoyed better access to healthcare. Americans are the most likely to skip a doctor’s appointment due to its cost, the most likely to skip a medical test and to skimp on prescription drugs . This is unsurprising, given the extraordinary lack of public health insurance in the United States. Americans face the highest out-of-pocket expenses for medical services in their peer group. source_signals.title: Welcome to Y’all Street: bullish Dallas aims to steal New York’s financial crown

Nestled between towers claimed by Bank of America and JP Morgan, Goldman Sachs has cordoned off 800,000 sq ft for a new Dallas campus able to host more than 5,000 staff. But the $700m (£530m) project is more than a regional expansion plan by one of America’s largest banks. It is another win for the lobbyists behind Dallas’s “Y’all Street” – the Texan city’s aggressive push to steal New York’s financial crown. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: DC’s highly qualified workers can’t find jobs: ‘What is happening?’
source_signals.summary: Washington DC has the highest unemployment rate in the US Alicia Contreras was in Tunisia, working as the deputy country representative for Libya for USAID, when she received the news: she was fired. The Trump administration had ceased the cooperation agency’s operations and terminated most overseas staff. What she didn’t expect back then was that after a double major, an MBA and 17 years of experience as a public servant, she wouldn’t be able to find a job back at home. Contreras moved back to the Washington DC area last September and immediately started her job search. She looked for positions in both the public and private sectors, in-person, hybrid and remote. She focused her search mostly on the US capital city and its two nearby states, Maryland and Virginia, because of her family commitments: she has two children, ages three and six. Six months later, none of her close to 100 applications have been successful. Continue reading...

But something was missing. “We were both comfortable but not happy with our jobs,” Janett said. “I felt unfulfilled and anxiously wanted to move forward.” But towards what, she wasn’t sure. source_signals.title: Financial bullying can ruin a marriage: first-person stories

Our readers, however, insist that that might be a label easily assigned to a partner who is simply being financially responsible. When we put out a call to out readers last week to see if any of them had similar experiences, we found that many of you wouldn’t define the actions described as financial bullying. source_signals.title: Tech oligarchs reshape humanity while billionaires of old seem quaint
source_signals.summary: From Gates to Musk and Altman, today’s ultra-rich steer AI and tech, raising questions about who decides the future When Bill Gates became the first modern IT mogul to reach the apex of wealth and power in 1992, the world was a very different place. Gates joined the top 10 on Forbes magazine’s billionaires list alongside Japanese, German, Canadian, South Korean and Swedish billionaires, including those with family fortunes from Britain and America. A broad mix of industries was on the list: Retail and media, property management and packaging, an investment firm and a couple of industrial conglomerates. Their fortunes almost added up to $100bn – equivalent to about 0.4% of the US’s GDP that year. The oligarchy has changed drastically since then. Bernard Arnault, of French luxury group LVMH, Amancio Ortega, the Spanish clothing mogul, and Warren Buffett, the US investor, were the only old-school billionaires among the top 10 in 2025. The rest largely made their money from high-tech: Elon Musk, Jeff Bezos, Mark Zuckerberg, Larry Ellison, Steve Ballmer and Google’s Sergey Brin and Larry Page. The top 10 amassed over $16trn, which is about 8% of US GDP. Continue reading...

The findings – on the basis of contracts worth £12bn – have prompted claims of “scandalous” profiteering, concern that the health service is being “taken for a ride” and calls for ministers to impose a cap on maximum profit levels. £2bn of the £12bn of contracts went to firms with owners based outside the UK. £533m of that £2bn went to companies owned by people living in tax havens such as Jersey and the Cayman Islands. Firms, especially those owned by private equity outfits, used £353m of their £12bn NHS income to pay interest on debts. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Woman, 19, killed in Essex dog attack named as Jamie-Lea Biscoe
source_signals.summary: Police arrest man, 37, on suspicion of being in charge of dog dangerously out of control and causing injury resulting in death A 19-year-old woman who died after a dog attack in Essex has been named by police as Jamie-Lea Biscoe. Police said the victim was found with serious injuries after emergency services were called to a property in Leaden Roding at 10.45pm on Friday. Biscoe was pronounced dead at the scene. A 37-year-old man from Dunmow, who was arrested on suspicion of being in charge of a dog dangerously out of control and causing injury resulting in death, has been bailed until July while inquiries continue, Essex police said on Sunday. The canine, which was a family pet and believed to be a lurcher crossbreed, was seized and tests are under way to formally establish the dog’s breed, the force added. Assistant chief constable Stuart Hooper said: “Our thoughts remain with all those who knew and loved Jamie-Lea. Her young life has been so tragically cut short. “Our detectives are continuing to work around the clock to establish exactly what happened and specialist officers are continuing to support Jamie-Lea’s family. “This is unimaginable for her loved ones and friends and, as such, I would ask people to respect their grief and privacy at this extremely difficult time. “Our officers remain at the scene and anyone with concerns or information can speak with them there or contact us in the usual way.” A post-mortem examination is due to take place on Sunday, police said. Anyone with information that could assist the investigation has been asked to contact Essex police through their website or anonymously through Crimestoppers. On Thursday, a three-month-old baby died in a suspected dog attack at a property in Redcar, North Yorkshire. The baby girl is believed to have died as a result of a dog bite in the Dormanstown area and a woman, aged 31, was treated in hospital for an injury to her arm from a bite, police said. Armed officers destroyed one dog that had gone on to the street and a second recovered by police has since been destroyed. A man, aged 45, was arrested on suspicion of being in charge of a dog dangerously out of control causing injury resulting in death and was released on conditional bail. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: ‘It feels as if I’ve made a new best friend’: my experiment with AI journalling
source_signals.summary: What’s it like to have a diary that talks back to you, offering comments and advice on your hopes, fears and lunch plans? I spent two months finding out Ever since I was a teenager, I have kept some form of diary . These days I favour a paper one for creative brainstorming, and the Journal app on my iPad where I do a speedily typed brain dump every morning. I have always found it a great way to impose some sort of order on my random thoughts, a form of meditation. But I had never even heard of AI journalling until a Google search led me down a rabbit hole where I encountered people enthusing about two apps, Rosebud and Mindsera. It sounded as if Mindsera’s minimalist design was the best for writers. Out of curiosity, never intending to stick with it, I downloaded a free trial. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Dr TikTok: patients diagnose chronic illnesses with anonymous commenters’ help
source_signals.summary: TikTok users increasingly say the app has steered them toward diagnosing medical problems not yet identified Malina Lee, a 31-year-old wedding baker based in San Antonio, Texas, joined TikTok during the Covid pandemic lockdowns in 2020. Like many people at the time, she was bored and began using the platform to pass the time and advertise her business. She didn’t expect a cancer diagnosis. Four years after Lee joined the app, a commenter with the username “PickleFart” told her that her neck looked asymmetrical in a way that could suggest she had a goiter – an enlarged thyroid gland – and that she should get it checked out. The anonymous amateur clinician turned out to be right – Lee had thyroid cancer, received treatment quickly, and, less than a year later, was cancer free. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Is AI the greatest art heist in history?

Around the globe, chatbots induce schizophrenic delusions and urge teens to kill themselves – all while turning users brains to mush . Who could have predicted this? Artists, that’s who. source_signals.title: ‘Irresponsible failure’: Google, Meta, Snap and Microsoft slam EU over child sexual abuse law lapse
source_signals.summary: Experts warn lapse could sharply reduce reports of abuse, echoing a 58% drop during a similar legal gap in 2021 Sign up for the Breaking News US email to get newsletter alerts in your inbox The European parliament has blocked the extension of a law that permits big tech firms to scan for child sexual exploitation on their platforms, creating a legal gap that child safety experts say will lead to crimes going undetected. The law, which was a carve-out of the European Union’s ePrivacy Directive, was put in place in 2021 as a temporary measure allowing companies to use automated detection technologies to scan messages for harms, including child sexual abuse material (CSAM), grooming and sextortion . However, it expired on 3 April, and the EU parliament decided not to vote to extend it, amid privacy concerns from some lawmakers. This article was amended on 10 April 2026 to correct the name of the EU’s ePrivacy Directive. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Elon Musk’s xAI sues Colorado over new rules for artificial intelligence
source_signals.summary: Company claims law regulating AI systems, set to go into effect in June, infringes on its first amendment rights Elon Musk ’s artificial intelligence company, xAI, has filed a lawsuit against the state of Colorado over a new AI law set to take effect in June. The suit seeks to block the state from enforcing the law, which would impose new requirements on AI systems to protect state residents from “algorithmic discrimination” in sectors such as education, employment, healthcare, housing and financial services. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: AI companies know they have an image problem. Will funding policy papers and thinktanks dig them out?
source_signals.summary: The aggressive effort by major players aims to reshape the narrative as polls show increasing public disapproval of AI OpenAI made a surprise announcement this week – not an update to ChatGPT or another multibillion-dollar datacenter – but a policy paper that called for a reimagining of the social contract based around “a slate of people-first ideas”. It’s the latest move in an aggressive effort by the major AI players to reshape the narrative around their industry, as polls show public disapproval of AI increasing. OpenAI’s 13-page paper, titled Industrial Policy for the Intelligence Age , follows its surprise acquisition of tech-friendly podcast TBPN and its announcement of plans to open a Washington DC office that will feature a dedicated space called the OpenAI workshop for non-profits and policymakers to learn about and discuss the company’s technology. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Super Mario what?! The seven best obscure Mario games
source_signals.summary: As The Super Mario Galaxy Movie storms the box office, we look back at the best forgotten games inspired by Tetris, Lemmings and … vitamins? It should be no surprise that the latest Super Mario movie is smashing box office records – despite the, let’s say mixed, reviews . Nintendo’s iconic plumber has been a pop culture staple for 45 years, starring in some of the bestselling video games ever made, from the original Donkey Kong through to the joyous Super Mario Bros Wonder and the chaotic Mario Kart World. But as with any storied showbiz career, there have been some lesser works. Who can forget – or actually remember – Hotel Mario, a door-shutting puzzle game for the doomed Philips CD-i console? Or what about Mario Teaches Typing, a 1992 educational game for the PC in which players navigate the Mushroom Kingdom by … correctly inputting words. Yet there have also been genuine treasures lost along the way. Here, then, are seven of our favourite much-overlooked Mario odysseys. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Rory McIlroy holds nerve to be the Master again as rivals succumb to tension
source_signals.summary: 2025 winner joins Nicklaus, Faldo and Woods in retaining title He triumphs on 12 under by one shot from Scottie Scheffler You are left wondering how on earth Augusta National managed to inflict such psychological torture on Rory McIlroy for all those years. Or maybe that is precisely the point, that ­McIlroy’s ending of his Masters hoodoo in 2025 placed him into a fresh head space where failure is not an option. It turns out Green Jackets are like London buses. Back in Augusta, where he became only the sixth man in history to complete a career grand slam, McIlroy entered the record books once more. He is now the fourth golfer to successfully defend the Masters, after Jack ­Nicklaus, Nick Faldo and Tiger Woods. As a six-time major winner, he has surpassed Seve Ballesteros. What next, Rory? He could walk on Rae’s Creek. McIlroy’s latest Masters triumph arrived with the 36-year-old considerably short of his best for much of the tournament. That only emphasises his excellence. Continue reading...

He had just outclassed Arslanbek Makhmudov, the tough but limited Russian heavyweight, over 12 one‑sided rounds. at_a_glance: Orbán’s 16-year rule in Hungary is over after Péter Magyar’s election landslide.
The US-Iran confrontation is deepening, with a blockade threat lifting oil above $100 and rattling markets.
The UK says it will not join any blockade of Iran’s ports, even as pressure rises over Hormuz.
Rory McIlroy has defended his Masters title, while Manchester City’s title push and Tottenham’s collapse reshape the football picture.
Labour is preparing a new EU-alignment law that could let ministers adopt single-market rules without a Commons vote.
Schools in England are set for the biggest food-standards overhaul in 13 years, targeting fried and sugary items.
AI-related regulation and trust issues continue to spread across business, politics and technology, from data centres to online safety.
Asha Bhosle’s death marks the end of a major era in Indian popular music, though the broader cultural significance sits below the top global stories.

lead_story.title: Orbán era swept away by Péter Magyar's Hungary election landslide
lead_story.summary: Viktor Orbán’s 16-year rule has ended after a decisive defeat by Péter Magyar, an ex-party insider who persuaded a majority of voters to turn the page. The result is a major shift for Hungary and for Europe, with implications for the rule of law, relations with Brussels and the wider nationalist right.
lead_story.source_signal_title: Orbán era swept away by Péter Magyar's Hungary election landslide
body: Viktor Orbán’s long hold on power has been broken after Péter Magyar led the opposition to a landslide that swept away the governing order built over more than a decade and a half. The scale of the defeat leaves Orbán with little room to argue that this was a narrow protest vote; it was a clear instruction from a weary electorate to move on.

Magyar’s rise has been remarkable because he is not a conventional opposition veteran. An ex-insider from Orbán’s political orbit, he managed to present himself as both credible and disruptive, turning disillusionment with corruption allegations, state capture and democratic backsliding into a broad coalition for change.

The result matters well beyond Budapest. Orbán has been one of Europe’s most stubborn nationalist leaders, repeatedly clashing with Brussels over the rule of law, judicial independence and migration policy, while also offering a model for other populist right-wing movements across the continent.

For the European Union, the change could be significant. A new government in Hungary may be more open to compromise on funding, institutional checks and relations with the bloc, though the extent of any reset will depend on how quickly Magyar can translate election momentum into governing authority and whether the old networks resist change.

Hungary’s political system was remade under Orbán through control of institutions, media influence and a hard-edged culture-war politics. Undoing that will not be simple, even with a clear electoral mandate, because entrenched interests and a polarised electorate can outlast a single election.

The next test is whether Magyar can turn a symbolic victory into stable government. He inherits high expectations at home, strained relations with allies and a country whose politics have been shaped for years by confrontation with Brussels and by Orbán’s appeal to nationalist voters.

stories.title: Trump's threat raises risks and leaves predicaments unchanged
stories.summary: The confrontation with Iran has become a test of wills, with Tehran’s capacity to absorb strikes now being measured against Donald Trump’s tolerance for the costs of a widening war.
stories.source_signal_title: Trump's threat raises risks and leaves predicaments unchanged
body: Donald Trump’s threat to escalate pressure on Iran has sharpened the risks in a confrontation that is now being measured as much by political endurance as by military capability. The immediate question is no longer whether both sides can inflict damage, but how much each is prepared to absorb before widening the conflict further.

Iran can still absorb considerable punishment and has shown in the past that it can respond asymmetrically, through regional proxies, shipping disruption and missile or drone attacks. That makes the military picture dangerous even when neither side appears to want a full-scale war.

For Trump, the calculation is different but no less fraught. Escalation may appeal as a show of resolve, yet any prolonged conflict would expose the United States to higher energy prices, retaliation against partners and the risk of being drawn into another Middle Eastern war with no clear exit.

The result is a familiar impasse. Pressure may change the tone of the standoff, but it does not solve the underlying dispute over Iran’s nuclear programme, regional influence or Washington’s demand for concessions. That is why this remains less a breakthrough than a test of nerve, with very little evidence that either side has found a way to step back.

stories.title: After Iran talks falter, the big question is what happens next?
stories.summary: The weekend’s failed diplomacy has not resolved the underlying conflict. The likely next steps include military escalation, disruption to shipping and renewed pressure on allies to clarify their position.
stories.source_signal_title: After Iran talks falter, the big question is what happens next?
body: The collapse of weekend talks has not settled the dispute between Washington and Tehran; it has simply moved the crisis into a more volatile phase. Diplomacy failed to deliver agreement, and that leaves the region exposed to the next move, which could come from either side or from their regional allies.

The most immediate danger is military escalation, whether through further strikes, retaliatory attacks or actions aimed at disrupting shipping in the Gulf. Any serious interference with the Strait of Hormuz would quickly spread beyond the two protagonists, affecting oil markets, insurers, ports and consumers far from the conflict zone.

Allies are also being forced into a position they would rather avoid. European governments, Gulf states and others with forces or trade interests in the region will come under pressure to clarify how far they are prepared to support deterrence, protect shipping and restrain a conflict that is already broadening in economic terms.

stories.title: Ukraine and Russia accuse each other of hundreds of ceasefire violations
stories.summary: The fragile ceasefire is fraying almost as soon as it takes hold, with both sides accusing the other of hundreds of breaches and warning of reciprocal responses.
stories.source_signal_title: Ukraine and Russia accuse each other of hundreds of ceasefire violations
body: The fragile ceasefire between Ukraine and Russia is fraying almost as soon as it takes hold, with each side accusing the other of hundreds of violations. The result is a familiar pattern: a brief pause in fighting, followed by mutual claims of bad faith and warnings that any further breach will be answered in kind.

Volodymyr Zelensky has said Ukraine would respond symmetrically to Russian attacks, underlining how little trust exists between the two sides. That matters because even a limited truce depends on some level of restraint and communication, both of which remain in short supply after years of war.

The accusations also suggest that the ceasefire may be more a tactical pause than a step towards negotiations. If the breaches continue, the arrangement could collapse altogether, making it harder for outside powers to argue that diplomacy can still contain the war without stronger guarantees or enforcement.

stories.title: Pope prioritises world's fastest-growing Catholic region in major Africa tour
stories.summary: Leo XIV’s Africa tour is intended to redirect attention to the continent where Catholicism is expanding most quickly, and where the Church’s long-term future may be decided.
stories.source_signal_title: Pope prioritises world's fastest-growing Catholic region in major Africa tour
body: Pope Leo XIV’s Africa tour is designed to shift the Church’s gaze towards the continent where Catholicism is expanding most quickly. In demographic terms, Africa is now central to the Church’s future, even if it still receives less attention than Europe or Latin America in much of Catholic public life.

The trip is therefore not only pastoral but strategic. It signals that the Vatican sees the Church’s centre of gravity moving southwards, with Africa’s growing congregations, seminaries and lay communities likely to play a larger role in shaping Catholicism’s next generation.

The visit also comes at a moment when the Church is trying to balance global diversity with institutional unity. How Leo XIV frames African priorities — from social justice and conflict to poverty and governance — will be watched closely for clues about the direction of his papacy and the voice he intends the Church to have on the world stage.

stories.title: Civilians feared killed after reports of air strike on Nigerian market
stories.summary: Reports of a strike on a village market have raised fears of civilian deaths, while the Nigerian Air Force has yet to address the allegations directly.
stories.source_signal_title: Civilians feared killed after reports of air strike on Nigerian market
body: There are growing fears that civilians have been killed after reports of an air strike on a village market in north-east Nigeria. The Nigerian Air Force has not yet directly addressed the allegation, leaving the scale of the incident and the number of casualties uncertain.

The reported target was militants, but markets in conflict zones often sit close to civilian life and can become the scene of devastating mistakes. If the reports are confirmed, the incident would add to longstanding concerns about the risks posed by military operations in areas where armed groups and local communities are closely intermingled.

What happens next will depend on whether the authorities acknowledge a misfire, open an investigation and provide a credible account of what occurred. In a region already scarred by years of insurgency, transparency matters not just for public confidence but for the protection of civilians in future operations.

stories.title: At least 30 feared dead in crush at Haitian tourist site
stories.summary: A crush at a crowded tourist event in Haiti has left at least 30 people feared dead, underlining the country’s continuing public-safety and governance crisis.
stories.source_signal_title: At least 30 feared dead in crush at Haitian tourist site
body: At least 30 people are feared dead after a crush at a crowded tourist event in Haiti, in a tragedy that once again exposes the country’s deep public-safety failings. The incident reportedly involved young people at a popular gathering site, and the number of injured may still rise.

The disaster lands in a country already struggling with weak institutions, poor emergency response and chronic insecurity. In Haiti, even routine crowd management can become difficult when the state lacks the capacity to regulate large events or respond quickly when panic breaks out.

The immediate priority is rescue and identification, but the broader significance is harder to ignore. Haiti’s governance crisis is not abstract: it shapes whether roads are safe, whether public events can be policed and whether families can expect even basic protection in moments of danger.

stories.title: Oil back above $100 as US to blockade Iranian ports after peace talks fail
stories.summary: The failure of negotiations and the threat of a blockade have pushed oil back above $100, intensifying fears that the global energy shock will deepen and spread further through prices.
stories.source_signal_title: Oil back above $100 as US to blockade Iranian ports after peace talks fail
body: Oil prices have climbed back above $100 after the collapse of peace talks and Donald Trump’s threat to blockade Iranian ports intensified fears of a deeper energy shock. The market reaction reflects not only the risk of lost supply, but the possibility that shipping routes in and around the Gulf could become much harder to use safely.

The Strait of Hormuz is central to global energy flows, so any move to restrict access immediately affects expectations for crude, refined products and freight. Even the prospect of disruption is enough to push prices higher, because traders are trying to price in not just lost barrels but delays, insurance costs and the risk of retaliation.

For consumers and central banks, the danger is that the shock spreads beyond oil. Higher energy costs can feed into transport, food and manufacturing prices, while also putting pressure on inflation and interest-rate expectations in economies that had only recently started to stabilise.

The main uncertainty is how far this confrontation goes. If the blockade threat is acted on, or if Iran responds by targeting shipping, the price spike could persist and widen. If diplomacy somehow resumes, markets may ease, but the episode has already shown how quickly geopolitics can reach the pump and the balance sheet.

stories.title: Collapse of US-Iran talks heightens fears of prolonged energy shock
stories.summary: The market reaction is now broader than oil alone: shipping, borrowing costs and consumer inflation are all being pulled into the same crisis as tankers remain stranded and policy responses harden.
stories.source_signal_title: Collapse of US-Iran talks heightens fears of prolonged energy shock
body: The failure of the talks has turned a sharp oil market reaction into something broader and potentially longer-lasting. This is no longer just about the crude price: shipping, borrowing costs and inflation expectations are all being pulled into the same crisis as tankers remain stranded and governments harden their positions.

Markets dislike uncertainty, and the present uncertainty is unusually wide. If tanker traffic stays constrained in the Gulf, companies will face higher freight and insurance bills, lenders may reassess risk, and governments will have to decide whether to release strategic reserves or accept higher consumer prices.

That is why the shock may persist even if headline prices ease a little. Once supply chains are disrupted, the effect works through contracts, inventory decisions and policy responses. The question now is not only how much oil is lost, but how long the wider economic aftershock lasts.

stories.title: Soaring pump prices drive US inflation to highest level in almost two years
stories.summary: US inflation has jumped to 3.3% as the Iran war feeds through to fuel costs, giving policymakers and consumers a fresh reminder of how quickly geopolitical shocks reach household budgets.
stories.source_signal_title: Soaring pump prices drive US inflation to highest level in almost two years
body: US inflation has risen to 3.3%, with higher pump prices helping push the rate to its strongest level in almost two years. The jump is a reminder of how fast a geopolitical shock can move from the Gulf to American households.

Fuel costs feed directly into transport spending and indirectly into a wide range of goods and services, so the impact is not limited to drivers filling up their cars. Policymakers will now be more alert to the risk that energy-driven price rises become more persistent if the conflict continues.

For the Federal Reserve, the timing is awkward. A fresh inflation pulse reduces room for comfort on interest rates, while consumers face another squeeze on real incomes. The big uncertainty is whether this is a one-off shock or the start of a more durable rise in prices if supply conditions worsen further.

stories.title: EU airline industry warns of fuel shortages if Strait of Hormuz stays closed
stories.summary: European airlines are warning of tighter jet-fuel availability if the Strait of Hormuz remains closed, raising the risk of travel disruption and wider pressure on transport costs.
stories.source_signal_title: EU airline industry warns of fuel shortages if Strait of Hormuz stays closed
body: European airlines are warning that jet fuel supplies could tighten if the Strait of Hormuz remains closed. That would matter not only for aviation but for the wider transport sector, where fuel availability and price stability are already under pressure from the Middle East crisis.

Airlines operate on thin margins and rely on predictable fuel flows, so even temporary shortages can force costly changes to schedules, refuelling plans and ticket prices. The warning is therefore as much about operational resilience as it is about headline fuel costs.

If the route stays shut, the effects could spread quickly through Europe’s travel network. A prolonged disruption would raise the risk of cancellations, route changes and higher costs for passengers and freight, making the Strait of Hormuz a direct issue for the continent’s summer travel and trade plans.

stories.title: Interest in EVs surges in Europe as fuel prices jump after Iran war
stories.summary: Rising petrol prices are already shifting consumer behaviour, with online interest in electric vehicles surging across several European markets.
stories.source_signal_title: Interest in EVs surges in Europe as fuel prices jump after Iran war
body: Rising fuel prices are already changing consumer behaviour in Europe, with online interest in electric vehicles surging in several markets. That does not mean drivers are ready to switch overnight, but it does show how sensitive car buyers remain to the cost of petrol and diesel.

Energy shocks often accelerate existing trends, and the latest spike may give fresh momentum to EV consideration even among households that had previously delayed the move. For manufacturers and dealers, that could reshape short-term demand patterns, particularly for smaller and more affordable models.

The broader point is that geopolitics can alter technology adoption as well as inflation. If fuel prices remain elevated, the case for electric vehicles becomes stronger for some buyers, while governments may also feel renewed pressure to support charging infrastructure and reduce the cost barriers to switching.

stories.title: Will UK petrol and diesel prices start going down?
stories.summary: Drivers are waiting to see whether the ceasefire and any stabilisation in supply translate into lower prices at the pump, though the pass-through to consumers may remain uneven.
stories.source_signal_title: Will UK petrol and diesel prices start going down?
body: UK drivers are waiting to see whether the latest ceasefire and signs of supply stabilisation translate into lower petrol and diesel prices. The answer is likely to be uneven, because retail prices do not always move in step with wholesale markets and suppliers can be slow to pass on reductions.

For motorists, that means relief at the pump may arrive later than the headline diplomatic news suggests. Prices are shaped not only by crude costs but by exchange rates, distribution charges and commercial decisions made by retailers and refiners.

The immediate question is whether lower wholesale costs are sustained long enough to filter through. If the supply route remains fragile, any drop may prove temporary; if tensions ease further, households could see some relief, though probably not as quickly or as sharply as they would hope.

stories.title: UK could adopt EU single market rules under new legislation
stories.summary: Sir Keir Starmer is planning a law that would let the government adopt EU single market rules without them being voted on in Parliament, signalling a major shift in post-Brexit economic policy.
stories.source_signal_title: UK could adopt EU single market rules under new legislation
body: Sir Keir Starmer is planning legislation that would allow the government to adopt EU single market rules without taking them through Parliament in the usual way. That would mark a notable shift in post-Brexit economic policy, even if ministers present it as a practical rather than ideological change.

The appeal for government is clear: closer regulatory alignment could reduce friction for exporters and make it easier to remove barriers in selected sectors. But the constitutional significance is equally obvious, because handing ministers greater latitude to follow Brussels rules will revive arguments about scrutiny, sovereignty and democratic oversight.

The debate will also be political, not just procedural. Supporters will argue that the UK needs flexibility to improve trade and competitiveness; critics will say it amounts to rule-taking by another name. What happens next will depend on how wide the legislation is drawn and how much Parliament is prepared to resist it.

stories.title: British Steel 'needs nationalising by the summer'
stories.summary: As ownership talks continue, pressure is building for state intervention to secure British Steel’s future, with nationalisation being cast as the likeliest outcome.
stories.source_signal_title: British Steel 'needs nationalising by the summer'
body: Pressure is mounting for state intervention in British Steel, with one Labour MP arguing that nationalisation may be needed by the summer. The comment reflects growing concern that private ownership talks may not deliver a stable long-term future for the company.

British Steel has long sat at the centre of a wider question about whether strategic heavy industry can survive without direct government support. Steelmaking is capital-intensive, exposed to global competition and politically sensitive because of the jobs, supply chains and regional identity tied to it.

Nationalisation is not yet a done deal, but it is increasingly being discussed as the likeliest way to secure operations and preserve capacity. The immediate uncertainty is whether ministers will move quickly enough to avoid a damaging delay, and whether they are prepared to accept the cost and political burden of ownership.

stories.title: OpenAI pauses UK data centre deal over energy costs and regulation
stories.summary: The pause is a setback for the UK’s hopes of attracting major AI investment, and a reminder that power prices and regulatory certainty are now central to technology expansion.
stories.source_signal_title: OpenAI pauses UK data centre deal over energy costs and regulation
body: OpenAI’s pause on a major UK data centre deal is a setback for the government’s hopes of drawing more artificial intelligence investment into Britain. It also underlines a hard truth for the sector: access to power, planning certainty and predictable regulation now matter as much as ambition and branding.

Data centres are power-hungry by design, so energy costs can make or break a location decision. If the UK is more expensive or less certain than competing jurisdictions, firms can and will look elsewhere, particularly when AI infrastructure is expanding rapidly across Europe, the US and Asia.

The pause does not necessarily mean the project is dead, but it does show that the UK’s pitch as an AI hub will be judged on delivery. Unless ministers can address energy, grid capacity and regulatory clarity, similar deals may continue to stall or drift elsewhere.

stories.title: Private firms providing services to NHS made £1.6bn profit in two years, research finds
stories.summary: New research revives the long-running argument over outsourcing in the NHS, with MPs calling the level of profit extracted from public contracts “scandalous” and demanding tighter limits.
stories.source_signal_title: Private firms providing services to NHS made £1.6bn profit in two years, research finds
body: Private firms providing services to the NHS made £1.6bn in profit over two years, according to new research that has reignited the argument over outsourcing in the health service. The figure is likely to sharpen scrutiny of how public contracts are awarded and what the taxpayer receives in return.

Supporters of outsourcing say private providers can help the NHS manage demand and fill gaps in capacity. Critics argue that excessive profits drain money from frontline care and leave the health service dependent on commercial arrangements that are not always transparent or good value.

The political pressure is now on ministers to justify where the line is drawn between partnership and profit extraction. If MPs succeed in pushing for tighter limits, the debate could reshape how much of the NHS is delivered by outside firms and under what terms.

stories.title: Thousands of unpaid carers to face DWP repayment demands during overhaul
stories.summary: The government is starting a large audit of historical carer’s allowance cases, but many carers will continue to face repayment demands while the review proceeds.
stories.source_signal_title: Thousands of unpaid carers to face DWP repayment demands during overhaul
body: Thousands of unpaid carers are set to face repayment demands as the Department for Work and Pensions begins a wide-ranging review of historical carer’s allowance cases. The overhaul is intended to address past errors, but for many carers the immediate reality remains more letters, more stress and continuing uncertainty.

Carer’s allowance has long been criticised for its complexity and for the harsh consequences of accidental overpayment. Many recipients are on low incomes and were never trying to defraud the system, which is why repayment demands have caused such anger among campaigners and MPs.

The government’s review could eventually lead to a fairer system, but the process is likely to be slow and contested. The key question is whether ministers will combine the audit with clearer rules and more lenient treatment of those who were caught out by a system they did not understand.

stories.title: More than a fifth of UK’s ‘austerity children’ scarred by poverty, study says
stories.summary: A new study says child poverty has left a lasting mark on a large share of children born after 2013, linking the harm directly to years of welfare cuts.
stories.source_signal_title: More than a fifth of UK’s ‘austerity children’ scarred by poverty, study says
body: A new study says more than a fifth of children born after 2013 have been left with lasting scars from poverty, linking the damage to years of welfare cuts. The findings add fresh weight to the argument that austerity has had consequences that extend well beyond the immediate squeeze on household budgets.

Child poverty affects health, school readiness, emotional development and later life chances, so the impact can persist long after the original policy choices have been made. That is why the study is likely to resonate in the broader debate over whether Britain has done enough to shield children from economic hardship.

The political significance is clear: this is not only a question of past policy, but of what current governments are willing to do about it. Unless incomes rise and support improves, the cohort now growing up in poverty may carry those effects into adulthood.

stories.title: Deep-fried food banned in new plans for school dinners
stories.summary: The government is tightening school-food rules in England, with fresh limits on fried and sugary items as part of its drive to curb childhood obesity.
stories.source_signal_title: Deep-fried food banned in new plans for school dinners
body: England’s school food rules are being tightened again, with new plans to restrict deep-fried and sugary items in school dinners. Ministers say the aim is to improve children’s diets and tackle obesity, a problem that has become central to public health policy.

The change will affect caterers, schools and families used to a broader range of menu choices, but it reflects a longstanding shift towards more vegetables, whole grains and less processed food. The government’s challenge is to improve nutrition without making meals less appealing or more expensive to provide.

This is likely to remain contentious, because school dinners sit at the intersection of health, cost and parental expectations. If the changes are implemented carefully, they could improve diet quality; if not, they risk becoming another rule that schools struggle to deliver in practice.

stories.title: UK will not join Trump's blockade of Iran's ports in the Strait of Hormuz
stories.summary: Britain says it will not join any US blockade of Iranian ports, even as it continues to operate minesweepers and anti-drone capabilities in the region.
stories.source_signal_title: UK will not join Trump's blockade of Iran's ports in the Strait of Hormuz
body: Britain has said it will not join any US blockade of Iranian ports in the Strait of Hormuz, even as it continues to operate minesweepers and anti-drone capabilities in the region. The position signals support for maritime security without endorsing a move that could be seen as escalating the conflict.

The Strait of Hormuz is one of the world’s most important shipping lanes, so any blockade would have immediate consequences for energy markets and global trade. By refusing to take part, the UK is drawing a line between protecting navigation and participating in coercive action against Iran.

The next issue is whether other allies follow suit or decide to align more closely with Washington. For now, Britain’s stance suggests caution: it wants the route reopened and shipping protected, but without being pulled into a direct confrontation that could widen the crisis.

stories.title: Dancer with MND performs on stage again through digital avatar
stories.summary: Brainwave and avatar technology has allowed a dancer with motor neurone disease to return to the stage, showing the growing reach of assistive digital tools.
stories.source_signal_title: Dancer with MND performs on stage again through digital avatar
body: A dancer living with motor neurone disease has returned to the stage through digital avatar technology, in an example of how rapidly assistive tools are advancing. The project used brainwave-linked systems to recreate movement and expression, allowing performance to continue in a new form.

The significance is wider than a single performance. As health and disability technology improves, it is opening up possibilities that were once unimaginable, from communication aids to more sophisticated forms of creative participation for people with severe physical limitations.

That said, the technology also raises questions about access, cost and how far digital substitutes can or should stand in for the human body on stage. Even so, the performance demonstrates the growing practical reach of tools that can restore agency as well as function.

stories.title: AI companies know they have an image problem. Will funding policy papers and thinktanks dig them out?
stories.summary: The industry is moving from product launches to persuasion, investing in policy work and think-tanks as public concern about AI grows.
stories.source_signal_title: AI companies know they have an image problem. Will funding policy papers and thinktanks dig them out?
body: Artificial intelligence companies are increasingly aware that they have an image problem. After years of product launches and bold claims, the industry is now investing in policy papers, research groups and think-tank work in an effort to shape the public debate around AI.

This is partly a response to growing anxiety about jobs, bias, safety and concentration of power. Firms that once sold themselves mainly on technological progress are now trying to persuade policymakers and the public that they can be responsible stewards of a fast-moving sector.

Whether this strategy works is uncertain. It may improve access in political circles, but it will not by itself resolve concerns about trust. The more AI reshapes work and information, the more its public reputation will depend on outcomes rather than messaging.

stories.title: ‘Irresponsible failure’: Google, Meta, Snap and Microsoft slam EU over child sexual abuse law lapse
stories.summary: Technology companies and child-safety experts warn that a legal gap in the EU could sharply reduce the detection of abuse material and grooming online.
stories.source_signal_title: ‘Irresponsible failure’: Google, Meta, Snap and Microsoft slam EU over child sexual abuse law lapse
body: Google, Meta, Snap and Microsoft have criticised a legal lapse in the EU that they say could weaken the detection of child sexual abuse material and grooming online. The companies argue the gap creates an unacceptable risk at a time when digital platforms remain central to how abuse is shared and hidden.

The warning also reflects the uneasy balance between privacy, encryption and child protection. Lawmakers have been trying to update rules for years, but disagreements over surveillance powers, scanning and enforcement have made it difficult to maintain a permanent legal framework.

What happens next will depend on whether Brussels can close the gap quickly and in a way that is workable for both regulators and platforms. If it cannot, experts fear the result will be fewer safeguards and more material slipping through the net.

stories.title: Brit says he is not elusive Bitcoin creator named by New York Times
stories.summary: A British computer scientist and entrepreneur has denied being Satoshi Nakamoto after the New York Times identified him as a candidate, reviving long-running speculation around Bitcoin’s origins.
stories.source_signal_title: Brit says he is not elusive Bitcoin creator named by New York Times
body: A British computer scientist and entrepreneur has denied that he is Satoshi Nakamoto after being identified by the New York Times as a possible Bitcoin creator. The denial does little to end one of technology’s longest-running mysteries, but it does reset the latest round of speculation.

The identity of Bitcoin’s founder matters less for the currency’s operation than for its mythology, yet the question continues to fascinate because it touches on the origins of a financial system that has outgrown its early underground culture. Any new claim inevitably invites scrutiny, scepticism and a fresh search for clues.

For now, the only solid fact is that the man in question says he is not the elusive inventor. The larger story is that Bitcoin’s founding mystery endures, and every new candidate adds to the mythology rather than closing the case.

stories.title: Rory McIlroy holds nerve to be the Master again as rivals succumb to tension
stories.summary: McIlroy has defended his Masters title, joining an elite group of repeat winners and confirming his place in golf’s modern record books.
stories.source_signal_title: Rory McIlroy holds nerve to be the Master again as rivals succumb to tension
body: Rory McIlroy has defended his Masters title at Augusta, confirming his place in golf’s modern record books and joining a very small group of repeat winners. The victory came after a tense final round in which he stayed calm while rivals stumbled under pressure.

The result matters because repeated success at Augusta is rare and because McIlroy’s stature in the game has now been reinforced in the most demanding way possible: by backing up a major triumph. It also adds to the sense that he remains one of the defining figures in men’s golf.

For his rivals, the message is clear. McIlroy still has the composure to win on the biggest stage, and the long chase for major consistency has only been strengthened by this defence. What comes next will be whether he can extend that form beyond Augusta.

stories.title: Man City smell blood - why Arsenal should fear title rivals
stories.summary: Manchester City’s recent surge has tightened the title race and left Arsenal under real pressure heading into the final phase of the season.
stories.source_signal_title: Man City smell blood - why Arsenal should fear title rivals
body: Manchester City’s recent surge has changed the feel of the title race. After Arsenal’s latest slip-up, Pep Guardiola’s side now look like a team sensing an opening rather than simply chasing one.

That matters because City know how to apply pressure over the closing stretch of a season. Arsenal still have the lead, but a rival that is winning convincingly and shrinking the gap with a game in hand changes the psychological landscape as much as the points table.

The danger for Arsenal is not only the arithmetic but the momentum. If City keep winning, every dropped point will feel heavier. The decisive question is whether Arsenal can steady themselves before the race becomes one of survival rather than control.

stories.title: Will Romero's tears become the enduring image of Tottenham's season?
stories.summary: Tottenham’s defeat at Sunderland leaves their season in danger of becoming defined by crisis, with relegation still a live possibility.
stories.source_signal_title: Will Romero's tears become the enduring image of Tottenham's season?
body: Cristian Romero’s tears after Tottenham’s defeat at Sunderland may become the lasting image of a season that is threatening to unravel. The result left Spurs in the relegation zone and intensified the sense that the club’s troubles have moved from bad form to genuine crisis.

The emotional reaction reflected more than one defeat. For a club of Tottenham’s size, being drawn into a survival fight would represent a severe collapse in expectation and a damaging blow to confidence, supporters and the new regime trying to steady results.

Whether Romero’s tears become symbolic will depend on what follows. If Tottenham recover quickly, the image may fade; if they do not, it may come to stand for a season defined by fear, instability and failure to respond when it mattered most.

stories.title: Van Aert beats Pogacar in thrilling Paris-Roubaix sprint
stories.summary: Wout van Aert has won Paris-Roubaix after a dramatic sprint finish against Tadej Pogacar, one of the season’s standout one-day races.
stories.source_signal_title: Van Aert beats Pogacar in thrilling Paris-Roubaix sprint
body: Wout van Aert has won Paris-Roubaix after a dramatic sprint finish against Tadej Pogacar, sealing one of the standout one-day races of the season. The victory came after a brutal contest in which both riders were pushed to the limit before the final decisive move.

The result matters because Paris-Roubaix rewards resilience as much as raw power. Beating a rider of Pogacar’s calibre in that setting underlines van Aert’s standing among cycling’s elite and adds another major line to his classics record.

For Pogacar, defeat in such a tight finish is no disgrace, but it will still sharpen the sense that the spring classics are becoming an even fiercer battleground. For van Aert, it is a prize that confirms he remains capable of winning the sport’s hardest and most unforgiving races.
